items.title: Biliary Atresia: Diagnostic and Treatment Approach
items.content: Biliary atresia presents as severe obstructive jaundice in infancy, with pale stools. Diagnosis is confirmed by surgical exploration and operative cholangiography after clinical suspicion based on laboratory and imaging findings.
keyPoints: Most common biliary anomaly in infants presenting with severe obstructive jaundice during the first month of life.
~10% of cases are treatable with Roux-en-Y choledocholejunostomy; Kasai procedure (hepatic portoenterostomy) is attempted for others to restore bile flow.
Even successful biliary-enteric anastomoses often lead to chronic cholangitis, hepatic fibrosis, and portal hypertension.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: Choledochal Cysts: Diagnosis and Risks
items.content: Choledochal cysts may present with symptoms after age 10. Diagnosis is made via ultrasound, CT, MRCP, or cholangiography. Only one-third of patients exhibit the classic triad of abdominal pain, jaundice, and an abdominal mass.
keyPoints: ~50% of patients present with onset of symptoms after age 10.
Ultrasonographic detection of a cyst separate from the gallbladder suggests choledochal cyst diagnosis.
Surgical treatment involves excision of the cyst and biliary-enteric anastomosis.
Patients are at increased risk for cholangiocarcinoma.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: When to Suspect CBD Stones in Cholecystitis
items.content: In patients presenting with cholecystitis, the presence of serum bilirubin levels greater than 85.5 μmol/L (5 mg/dL) should raise suspicion for common bile duct (CBD) stones.
keyPoints: Serum bilirubin level >85.5 μmol/L (5 mg/dL) in cholecystitis suggests CBD stones.
Maximum bilirubin level in choledocholithiasis is usually <256.5 μmol/L (15.0 mg/dL) unless other factors are present.
Serum bilirubin levels ≥342.0 μmol/L (20 mg/dL) suggest neoplastic obstruction.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Acute Cholangitis
items.content: Acute cholangitis requires prompt intervention with endoscopic or surgical procedures to relieve biliary obstruction and drain infected bile, as antibiotics alone are often insufficient.
keyPoints: 75% of acute cholangitis patients have bacteria in bile cultures early in the course.
Charcot's triad: biliary pain, jaundice, spiking fevers with chills.
Endoscopic sphincterotomy via ERCP is preferred for both diagnosis and treatment.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Obstructive Jaundice
items.content: Obstructive jaundice due to choledocholithiasis typically presents with painless jaundice, pruritus, and acholic stools, contrasting with malignancy-related obstruction.
keyPoints: Painless jaundice is more characteristic of malignancies than calculous disease.
Courvoisier's law: absence of a palpable gallbladder suggests malignancy over calculous disease.
Elevated serum alkaline phosphatase often precedes clinical jaundice.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Secondary Biliary Cirrhosis: Complications and Progression
items.content: Secondary biliary cirrhosis can develop from prolonged or intermittent duct obstruction, potentially leading to severe hepatic complications. Even after correcting the obstructing process, progression of cirrhosis may result in portal hypertension, hepatic failure, and death.
keyPoints: Complication seen with choledocholithiasis but more common with strictures
Progressive even post-correction of obstruction
Can lead to portal hypertension, hepatic failure, and death
Associated deficiencies in fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Choledocholithiasis
items.content: Choledocholithiasis is diagnosed via cholangiography. Preoperative ERCP with endoscopic papillotomy is preferred for suspected CBD stones before laparoscopic cholecystectomy, as it provides stone clearance and defines biliary anatomy. Intraoperative cholangiography can identify retained stones, leading to postoperative ERCP if necessary.
keyPoints: Diagnosis by cholangiography: ERC or MRCP
15% of cholecystectomy patients have CBD stones
Preoperative ERCP preferred for suspected CBD stones
Risk factors for suspecting CBD stones include history of jaundice/pancreatitis, abnormal liver function tests, and dilated CBD on imaging
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Benign Bile Duct Strictures Post-Cholecystectomy
items.content: Benign strictures in the extrahepatic bile ducts often result from surgical trauma, occurring in approximately 1 in 500 cholecystectomies. These may present with immediate post-operative complications such as bile leak or abscess formation, or later with biliary obstruction or cholangitis up to 2 years after surgery. Diagnosis is confirmed via percutaneous or endoscopic cholangiography, with endoscopic brushing providing more accurate lesion characterization than bile cytology alone.
keyPoints: Benign strictures occur in about 1 in 500 cholecystectomies.
Presentations include bile leak, abscess formation, biliary obstruction, or cholangitis up to 2 years post-surgery.
Diagnosis via percutaneous or endoscopic cholangiography; endoscopic brushing is more accurate than bile cytology alone.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS

items.subtopic: TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS
items.title: Recognition and Intervention for Hemobilia
items.content: Hemobilia can result from traumatic or operative injury to the liver or bile ducts, among other causes. Patients typically present with a triad of biliary pain, obstructive jaundice, and melena or occult blood in stools. Diagnosis may be confirmed by cholangiographic evidence of blood clots in the biliary tree, though selective angiographic verification might be necessary. While minor episodes can resolve without intervention, significant cases may require arteriography and transcatheter embolization or surgical ligation.
keyPoints: Hemobilia presents with biliary pain, obstructive jaundice, and melena/occult blood in stools.
Diagnosis via cholangiographic evidence of blood clots; selective angiographic verification may be needed.
Minor episodes might resolve without intervention; significant cases require arteriography or surgical ligation.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hapatobiliary Parasitism
items.content: Hepatobiliary parasitism involves infestation by helminths leading to chronic pyogenic cholangitis, hepatic abscesses, or biliary obstruction. Diagnosis is confirmed via cholangiography and stool examination for ova.
keyPoints: Common parasites include Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis viverrini/felineus, Fasciola hepatica, Ascaris lumbricoides, Echinococcus spp.
Diagnosis: Cholangiography and stool examination for characteristic ova
Treatment involves laparotomy with antibiotic coverage, common duct exploration, and biliary drainage
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Differentiating PSC from IgG4-Associated Cholangitis
items.content: PSC is associated with inflammatory bowel disease, while IgG4-associated cholangitis presents similarly but requires serum IgG4 level assessment for differentiation.
keyPoints: PSC: Strongly linked to ulcerative colitis
IgG4-associated cholangitis: Elevated serum IgG4 and IgG4-positive plasma cells in bile ducts/liver tissue
Initial treatment for IgG4 disease is glucocorticoids; steroid-sparing agents may be needed
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of PSC
items.content: PSC presents with chronic biliary obstruction symptoms, diagnosed by cholangiography showing multifocal strictures. MRCP is preferred for suspected cases.
keyPoints: Symptoms: RUQ pain, pruritus, jaundice, acute cholangitis
Late complications: Complete biliary obstruction, secondary cirrhosis, hepatic failure, portal hypertension
Diagnosis: Multifocal strictures with normal/dilated ducts on cholangiography; MRCP is the technique of choice
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Management of Pruritus and Complications in PSC
items.content: In Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC), there is no proven medical therapy for the disease itself. However, pruritus associated with PSC can be managed using medications such as cholestyramine, rifampin, fenofibrate/bezafibrate, sertraline, and naltrexone. Antibiotics are recommended when bacterial cholangitis complicates the clinical picture.
keyPoints: No proven medical therapy for PSC
Pruritus management: cholestyramine, rifampin, fenofibrate/bezafibrate, sertraline, naltrexone
Antibiotics useful in bacterial cholangitis
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

items.title: Intervention and Prognosis in PSC
items.content: For high-grade biliary obstruction (dominant strictures) in PSC, balloon dilatation is preferred over stenting due to a higher complication rate with stents, including pancreatitis and cholangitis. Surgical intervention is rarely indicated. The median survival following diagnosis of PSC is 12–18 years, regardless of therapy. Four variables predict survival: age, serum bilirubin level, histologic stage, and splenomegaly.
keyPoints: Balloon dilatation preferred over stenting for high-grade biliary obstruction
Surgical intervention rarely indicated
Median survival 12–18 years post-diagnosis
Survival predictors: age, serum bilirubin level, histologic stage, splenomegaly
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

items.question: Which diagnostic procedure confirms the diagnosis of biliary atresia?
items.explanation: The diagnosis of biliary atresia is confirmed by surgical exploration and operative cholangiography.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: What percentage of patients with choledochal cysts present with the classic triad of symptoms?
items.explanation: Only one-third of patients show the classic triad of abdominal pain, jaundice, and an abdominal mass.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: Which condition is associated with recurrent cholangitis and brown pigment gallstone formation?
items.explanation: In Caroli's disease, clinical manifestations include recurrent cholangitis and brown pigment gallstone formation.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: Which type of stones are most commonly found in bile ducts due to cholelithiasis?
items.explanation: The overwhelming majority of bile duct stones are cholesterol stones formed in the gallbladder.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic presentation of acute cholangitis?
items.explanation: Acute cholangitis typically presents with biliary pain, jaundice, and spiking fevers with chills (Charcot's triad).
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.question: What is Courvoisier's law related to?
options: High alkaline phosphatase levels indicate cholecystitis
items.explanation: Courvoisier's law states that a palpably enlarged gallbladder suggests biliary obstruction due to malignancy rather than calculous disease.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.question: What should be suspected in patients with cholecystitis and serum bilirubin >85.5 μmol/L?
items.explanation: CBD stones should be suspected in patients with cholecystitis whose serum bilirubin level is >85.5 μmol/L.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic method is preferred for identifying choledocholithiasis prior to laparoscopic cholecystectomy?
options: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiogram (ERC) with endoscopic papillotomy and stone extraction
Intraoperative cholangiography at the time of cholecystectomy
items.explanation: Preoperative ERCP with endoscopic papillotomy and stone extraction is preferred for identifying choledocholithiasis prior to laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.question: What complication may result from prolonged or intermittent duct obstruction in patients with choledocholithiasis?
items.explanation: Prolonged or intermittent duct obstruction may lead to secondary biliary cirrhosis.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for suspecting CBD stones in gallstone patients?
options: Ultrasonographic evidence of a dilated CBD or stones in the duct
items.explanation: A history of jaundice, pancreatitis, abnormal liver function tests, and ultrasonographic evidence of a dilated CBD or stones in the duct are risk factors for suspecting CBD stones.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS
items.question: Which diagnostic procedure is more accurate than bile cytology alone for establishing the nature of biliary strictures?
items.explanation: Endoscopic brushing is more accurate than bile cytology alone for establishing the nature of biliary strictures.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS

items.question: What is a common cause of extrinsic compression leading to obstructive jaundice?
options: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas
items.explanation: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is a common cause of extrinsic compression leading to obstructive jaundice.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS

items.question: What triad of symptoms often presents in patients with hemobilia?
options: Biliary pain, obstructive jaundice, and melena or occult blood in the stools
Weight loss, anorexia, and abdominal distension
Fever, chills, and right upper quadrant tenderness
items.explanation: Patients with hemobilia often present with a triad of biliary pain, obstructive jaundice, and melena or occult blood in the stools.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · TRAUMA, STRICTURES, AND HEMOBILIA | EXTRINSIC COMPRESSION OF THE BILE DUCTS

items.question: Which of the following organisms is most commonly involved in hepatobiliary parasitism leading to chronic pyogenic cholangitis?
options: Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis viverrini or Opisthorchis felineus, and Fasciola hepatica
items.explanation: The passage mentions that trematodes or flukes such as Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis viverrini or Opisthorchis felineus, and Fasciola hepatica are most commonly involved in hepatobiliary parasitism.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.question: What diagnostic procedure is used to establish the diagnosis of sclerosing cholangitis?
items.explanation: The diagnosis of sclerosing cholangitis is established by finding multifocal, diffusely distributed strictures with intervening segments of normal or dilated ducts, producing a beaded appearance on cholangiography.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.question: Which condition is strongly associated with inflammatory bowel disease, especially ulcerative colitis?
items.explanation: The passage states that PSC is strongly associated with inflammatory bowel disease, especially ulcerative colitis.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.question: What initial treatment is recommended for IgG4-associated cholangitis?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that glucocorticoids are the initial treatment of choice for IgG4-associated cholangitis.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.question: Which of the following treatments is preferred over stenting in cases of high-grade biliary obstruction due to PSC?
items.explanation: Balloon dilatation is preferred over stenting for high-grade biliary obstruction in PSC due to lower complication rates.

items.question: What is the median survival time following a diagnosis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis?
items.explanation: The median survival for PSC is 12–18 years following diagnosis.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a variable used in predicting survival in patients with PSC?
items.explanation: Liver enzyme levels are not mentioned as one of the variables for predicting survival in PSC.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

items.statement: Most patients with biliary atresia eventually develop chronic cholangitis and hepatic fibrosis.
items.explanation: Most patients, even those having successful biliary-enteric anastomoses, eventually develop chronic cholangitis, extensive hepatic fibrosis, and portal hypertension.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.statement: Choledochal cysts are most commonly diagnosed in infants.
items.explanation: ~50% of patients present with onset of symptoms after age 10, indicating that choledochal cysts are not most commonly diagnosed in infancy.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.statement: Acute cholangitis symptoms result from inflammation due to complete obstruction of bile flow.
items.explanation: Cholangitis results from at least partial obstruction leading to inflammation, often with bacteria present.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.statement: Endoscopic management of bacterial cholangitis is less effective than surgical intervention.
items.explanation: Endoscopic management, such as ERCP with endoscopic sphincterotomy, is as effective as surgical intervention for bacterial cholangitis.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.statement: Secondary biliary cirrhosis may progress even after correcting the obstructive process.
items.explanation: Once established, secondary biliary cirrhosis can be progressive despite correction of the obstruction.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.statement: The need for preoperative ERCP is expected to increase as laparoscopic techniques improve.
items.explanation: The need for preoperative ERCP is expected to decrease with the improvement of laparoscopic techniques.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 2)

items.statement: There is a proven medical therapy that can cure Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis.

items.assertion: Painless jaundice in choledocholithiasis suggests malignancy.

items.assertion: Acute pancreatitis is commonly associated with choledocholithiasis.
items.reason: Gallstones passing through the common duct are a common factor in both conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiogram (ERC) is used for both diagnosis and treatment in choledocholithiasis.
items.reason: Preoperative ERCP with endoscopic papillotomy and stone extraction provides stone clearance and defines the anatomy of the biliary tree.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ERC is used for both diagnosis and treatment.

items.assertion: All patients diagnosed with sclerosing cholangitis should have a serum IgG4 level checked.
items.reason: To rule out IgG4 disease as a cause of secondary sclerosing cholangitis, particularly if they do not have inflammatory bowel disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why serum IgG4 levels should be checked.
items.reference: DISEASES OF THE BILE DUCTS · HEPATOBILIARY PARASITISM | SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.assertion: Patients with AIDS-associated sclerosing cholangitis may present with pancreatitis.
items.reason: Biliary tract lesions in AIDS include infection and changes of sclerosing cholangitis, which can involve the pancreatic duct.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with AIDS-associated sclerosing cholangitis may present with pancreatitis.
